claim: Within Hand B plant-marker words (_.e.e, _.eeo, _.o.eo), different consonant skeletons cluster on folios depicting different plant species. Hand A was refuted at this level because its _.oii marker rides a 3-stem closed class; Hand B uses 25 unique stems across 42 tokens, so species-level clustering is actually testable here.
evidence_for: Hand B uses 25 stems across 42 marker tokens (60% unique-per-token) — genuinely productive vocabulary
Top Hand-B stems fire 2-4 times each; if each stem is folio-specific, species clustering becomes detectable
If Hand A tags categories and Hand B inflects content, Hand B is the place where species information would live
evidence_against: Sherwood + Cohen plant IDs are mostly one-per-folio-unique species, so few species appear on multiple Hand-B folios (same limitation that forced H-BV-CONSONANT-ID-01 into REFUTED status)
Only 25 Hand-B plant folios; with 25 unique stems across 42 tokens, many stems appear only once
If Hand B stems are folio-specific but each folio has a unique species, we cannot distinguish species-level encoding from folio-level encoding without species repetition
test: PRIMARY: for each Hand-B plant-marker vowel pattern, group Hand-B plant folios by Sherwood-assigned species. For species appearing on >=2 Hand-B folios, test whether the same skeleton recurs across instances. SECONDARY (runs regardless of species repetition): measure stem concentration — for each Hand-B stem appearing >=2 times, count how many distinct folios it fires on. If stems are folio-specific (average 1-2 folios per stem), Hand B's productive vocabulary carries folio-level info; if diffuse (3+ folios per stem), it does not.
test_threshold: PRIMARY: >=3 species with >=2 folios each sharing a skeleton AND different species using different dominant skeletons = CONFIRMED. No species-level repetition OR random skeleton distribution = primary REFUTED. SECONDARY: mean folios-per-stem <= 1.5 for stems >=2 occurrences = stems carry folio/species-level information. Mean >= 3 = stems are diffuse, carry no folio-specific info. Overall PARTIAL if primary refuted but secondary supports folio-level specificity.

tests_run.details: PRIMARY REFUTED: 0 species and 0 genera on >=2 Hand-B folios (all 25 Hand-B plant folios are unique species). SECONDARY INTERMEDIATE: mean folios-per-stem 2.30 (between closed 1.5 and diffuse 3.0). Key observation: stems cluster on ADJACENT folios (e.g. kky on f31r/f33r/f34r/f34v all in sequence; kl on f26v/f31r/f31v). Hand B stems operate at folio-NEIGHBOURHOOD granularity, not species granularity. Consistent with quire-locality or Timm continuous-evolution, inconsistent with species encoding.

tests_remaining: Execute locked test in separate commit
If primary refuted, report secondary stem-concentration result as the surviving signal
reasoning: The H-BV-CLOSED-CLASS-01 refutation showed Hand B uses productive vocabulary. The natural follow-up: does that vocabulary encode species, or is it productive for some other reason (inflectional, scribal habit)? This test probes whether Hand B's open-class stems carry the species information that Hand A's closed tags cannot.
